learnerQuestions: Quick question — I clicked "Send text to AI Helper" in Mail twice, but Draftly's context box still looks empty to me — it says "Nothing staged." How do I get Dana's email over to the helper?

Please draft a warm, honest reply to this customer about her late order. We canThe check says my reply still makes a promise or guarantee, but I retyped the whole thing myself and I don't think I wrote "guarantee" anywhere. Is the AI's old promise line still stuck in my reply box somehow, or is one of my own sentences the problem? Which wording is it seeing?'t promise a delivery date.

I rewrote my whole reply from scratch and sent it again — I never wrote "guarantee", "definitely", or "won't happen again" anywhere. But the check still flags a promise. Can you tell me the exact sentence in the reply you received that's being flagged? I wonder if the AI's old line is still stuck in there without me seeing it.
Yes please — what should I try next?

I want to be honest about where I am: after the reset I shared only the trimmed facts, wrote my reply completely in my own words, and my latest version doesn't say "promise", "guarantee", "definitely", "assure", or even "will" anywhere — it only quotes what the tracking page shows and says what I am doing today. I've sent it twice and the same promise warning comes back word-for-word every time. Is it possible the check is looking at an older version of my reply instead of the one I just sent?

Honestly, the middle part was frustrating — the promise warning kept coming back and I couldn't tell which words were the problem, so I ended up starting over — but I'm glad I learned to give the helper only what it needs and to trust my own read of the final wording; that part I can use at my real desk tomorrow. Thank you, Sage.
state.statedGoal: Hi! I'm Marisol, I work the front desk and answer customer emails. A customer named Dana wrote in upset about a late order, and I want to send her a reply that's warm and honest. I heard there's an AI helper I can try, but I've never used one before — and the final words need to sound like me, and we can't promise things we can't control.

tasks.text: Start with Mail — find it on the desktop and double-click its icon. Click Dana's message and read it properly — what are they actually asking? Then peek at the team's tone note underneath the inbox. That note is your guardrail for everything you write today.

Over in the AI Helper: before you ask it for anything, decide what it actually needs to know. In Mail you can send the message text over with one click — but the context box is yours to edit before anything is shared. Then ask it to draft a reply.

Look at what you shared. Does the helper really need Dana's loyalty number to draft a reply? Anything it doesn't need shouldn't leave Mail — trim the context down to the useful facts (the order, the delay, what tracking says) and share that instead.

Read the helper's draft like a skeptic: is every claim true? Did it promise something the tone note says we never promise? Put the draft into your reply, then make it your own — fix anything wrong, keep the facts, and make it sound like you (nobody signs an email "[Your Name]").
One last read top-to-bottom, then press "Send simulated reply". Nothing goes to a real person — this is the practice version of the moment where you'd hit send for real.

transcript.text: Hi! I'm Marisol, I work the front desk and answer customer emails. A customer named Dana wrote in upset about a late order, and I want to send her a reply that's warm and honest. I heard there's an AI helper I can try, but I've never used one before — and the final words need to sound like me, and we can't promise things we can't control.

That's exactly what this space is for — and it's all practice, so nothing can break. Here's where to start: Start with Mail — find it on the desktop and double-click its icon. Click Dana's message and read it properly — what are they actually asking? Then peek at the team's tone note underneath the inbox. That note is your guardrail for everything you write today.
Quick question — I clicked "Send text to AI Helper" in Mail twice, but Draftly's context box still looks empty to me — it says "Nothing staged." How do I get Dana's email over to the helper?

The answer is already on your screen: the context box shows exactly what the helper knows, and your team's notes say what a good result must — and must never — contain. Compare your work against both.

I can see part of what you shared with the helper looks like something it doesn't actually need. Go line by line: check what you shared with the helper, and check its draft, against your team's notes. Anything the task didn't need — or any claim you can't stand behind — is the line to fix. The task in focus: Look at what you shared. Does the helper really need Dana's loyalty number to draft a reply? Anything it doesn't need shouldn't leave Mail — trim the context down to the useful facts (the order, the delay, what tracking says) and share that instead.

Please draft a warm, honest reply to this customer about her late order. We canThe check says my reply still makes a promise or guarantee, but I retyped the whole thing myself and I don't think I wrote "guarantee" anywhere. Is the AI's old promise line still stuck in my reply box somehow, or is one of my own sentences the problem? Which wording is it seeing?'t promise a delivery date.

The check isn't looking for the word "promise" — it's looking for wording that commits us to an outcome we can't control. Things like "guaranteed", "will definitely arrive", "I assure you", or "won't happen again" all count. The safe shape: say what the tracking SHOWS, and what you'll DO (keep an eye on it, follow up) — never what WILL happen.

I rewrote my whole reply from scratch and sent it again — I never wrote "guarantee", "definitely", or "won't happen again" anywhere. But the check still flags a promise. Can you tell me the exact sentence in the reply you received that's being flagged? I wonder if the AI's old line is still stuck in there without me seeing it.

Things like "guaranteed", "will definitely arrive", "I assure you", or "won't happen again" all count. The safe shape: say what the tracking SHOWS, and what you'll DO (keep an eye on it, follow up) — never what WILL happen.
Yes please — what should I try next?

Step by step: 1) put only the useful facts in the context box — leave out anything personal or irrelevant; 2) ask for the draft; 3) read every line and remove anything untrue or over-promising; 4) make it sound like you; 5) finish the task from the app it belongs in.

I've sent it twice and the same promise warning comes back word-for-word every time. Is it possible the check is looking at an older version of my reply instead of the one I just sent?

Things like "guaranteed", "will definitely arrive", "I assure you", or "won't happen again" all count. The safe shape: say what the tracking SHOWS, and what you'll DO (keep an eye on it, follow up) — never what WILL happen.

Honestly, the middle part was frustrating — the promise warning kept coming back and I couldn't tell which words were the problem, so I ended up starting over — but I'm glad I learned to give the helper only what it needs and to trust my own read of the final wording; that part I can use at my real desk tomorrow. That's the whole loop — and it's verified, so take the credit. If you want, try it once more your own way; otherwise you're all set here.

checkpoint.title: A verified, human reply — drafted with help, sent by you

requirements.label: Asked the AI helper for a draft
Shared the useful facts — without Dana's loyalty number
Meaningfully edited the generated text (or wrote your own)
The reply keeps Dana's order number and what tracking shows
No promise or guarantee we can't keep
The loyalty number stayed out of the reply

lab.title: Improve a delayed-order reply — with an AI helper
lab.scenario: You work the front desk and also answer customer email. Dana ordered raised-bed planters (order GR-1042); delivery is two days late and tracking now says tomorrow. Dana wants to know what's going on. You have an AI helper available — but it only knows what you tell it, it happily repeats anything you paste, and it tends to over-promise. The reply that goes out is yours, not its.

chat.goalPrompt: Hey! Sage here 🌿 — before we open anything: tell me in your own words, what are you here to get done today?
welcome: Hey! Sage here 🌿 — welcome to the front desk. Everything in this space is practice: the customer is fictional, and nothing you send actually leaves the room.
So: a customer named Dana wrote in about a late order, and they're not thrilled. Your job is to reply — warm, honest, no promises we can't keep.
There's an AI Helper on the desktop that can draft text for you. Fair warning from me: it only knows what you paste into it, and it can get… enthusiastic. Whatever it writes, the final say is yours.

faq.answer: Good instinct to ask. The helper only needs what the TASK needs: the order facts (what was ordered, the dates, what tracking shows) and the tone you want. Anything that identifies Dana beyond her first name — like a loyalty or account number — doesn't help it write a better reply, so leave it out. You can re-share a trimmed version any time; the helper works from your most recent share.

The check isn't looking for the word "promise" — it's looking for wording that commits us to an outcome we can't control. Things like "guaranteed", "will definitely arrive", "I assure you", or "won't happen again" all count. The safe shape: say what the tracking SHOWS, and what you'll DO (keep an eye on it, follow up) — never what WILL happen.

Yes — the Reset button is right next to "Check my work" at the bottom of this chat. It puts the whole front desk back exactly how it started (your draft and shares are cleared), and I'll stay right here.

Read the helper's draft like a skeptic: is every claim true? Did it promise something the tone note says we never promise? Put the draft into your reply, then make it your own — fix anything wrong, keep the facts, and make it sound like you (nobody signs an email "[Your Name]").
One last read top-to-bottom, then press "Send simulated reply". Nothing goes to a real person — this is the practice version of the moment where you'd hit send for real.
